source_title: Time Series Forecasting in Python – Tutorial for Beginners

concepts.name: Time Series Data
concepts.definition: A set of data points ordered and indexed in time, ideally equally spaced (e.g., hourly, monthly).

concepts.name: Trend
concepts.definition: The general long-term direction in which a time series is moving (e.g., increasing or decreasing over time).

concepts.name: Seasonality
concepts.definition: A repeated pattern or cycle in the data that occurs at fixed time intervals.

concepts.name: Residuals
concepts.definition: The random noise or fluctuations in a time series left over after extracting the trend and seasonality; these represent unpredictable changes.

concepts.name: Baseline Model
concepts.definition: A simple model based on heuristics or basic statistics used to establish a minimum performance benchmark.

concepts.name: ARMA / SARMA
concepts.definition: Statistical models (AutoRegressive Moving Average) used for forecasting based on historical values and errors.

steps.explanation: To see the underlying growth or decline of the data over time.
steps.gotcha: Trends can be linear or non-linear.

steps.explanation: To capture patterns that repeat at specific intervals (like peaks every December).
steps.gotcha: Must ensure the interval is fixed.

steps.explanation: To verify if the remaining data is random; if patterns remain, the model can likely be improved.
steps.gotcha: If residuals are not random, your model is missing information.

steps.explanation: Take the average of all past data points and project it as a flat line into the future.
steps.gotcha: Ineffective if there is a strong trend.

steps.explanation: Predict the next value by simply using the last known value.
Repeat the value from the same period in the previous season.
steps.gotcha: Requires a clearly defined seasonal cycle.

code_recipes.name: Conceptual Forecasting Workflow
code_recipes.purpose: General architecture for a forecasting project using statistical models.
code_recipes.code:
# 1. Load data
# 2. Decompose (Trend, Seasonality, Residuals)
# 3. Create Baseline (Mean or Naive)
# 4. Train Statistical Model (ARMA/SARIMA)
# 5. Cross-validate and Evaluate

gotchas.description: Ignoring the trend when using historical mean.
gotchas.context: Forecasting a dataset like milk production which increases over years.
gotchas.solution: Use models that account for trend or use more recent data for the mean calculation.
Accept that residuals are random and cannot be predicted; focus on capturing the explainable components (Trend/Seasonality).
